sanskrit:
पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिनः।
तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्सबान्धवान्।
स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिनः स्याम माधव।।
transliteration:
pāpam evāśrayed asmān hatvaitān ātatāyinaḥ
tasmān nārhā vayaṁ hantuṁ dhārtarāṣṭrān sa-bāndhavān
sva-janaṁ hi kathaṁ hatvā sukhinaḥ syāma mādhava
literal_meaning: Sin alone will cling to us by slaying these aggressors; therefore we are not worthy to kill the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra along with their kin. How, after killing our own people, could we be happy, O Mādhava?
contextual_meaning: Arjuna projects the moral contamination of violence onto himself, collapsing personal identity with anticipated guilt and foreclosing any possibility of post-action wellbeing; he is caught in a cognitive-emotional bind where duty appears indistinguishable from self-annihilation. The verse exposes the moment when anticipated moral injury overrides warrior codes, revealing how the fear of becoming a perpetrator can paralyze necessary action and trap the mind in anticipatory shame.
psychological_state: A person frozen by the conviction that any decisive action will irreversibly brand them as evil, rendering future happiness impossible.
trigger_scenario: A whistle-blower paralyzed at the keyboard, convinced that exposing corporate abuse will destroy family relationships and leave them permanently ostracized and joyless.
anchor_text: Face necessary action without letting fear of future guilt freeze you.
